name: OS&SP Unit-1.pdf
textContent:
UNIT-1
 Operating Systems & System Programming
(23ACA06)
 

Operating System
•An operating system is an interface between a 
computer user and computer hardware. 
•An operating system is a software which performs all 
the basic tasks such as file management, memory 
mgmt, process mgmt, handling input and output, 
and controlling peripheral devices such as disk drives 
and printers.
•The software that contains the core components of 
the operating system is called thekernel.
2

Architecture of OS
3

Computer System Components
1.Hardware – provides basic computing resources (CPU, 
memory, I/O devices).
2. Operating system – controls and coordinates the use of the 
hardware among the various application programs for the 
various users.
3. Applications programs – The system resources are used to 
solve the computing problems of the users.
4. Users - (people, machines, other computers). Abstract View of 
System Components
4

Operating system goals
–Execute user programs and make solving user 
problems easier.
–Make the computer system convenient to use.
–Use the computer hardware in an efficient 
manner.
5

Functions of Operating System
i. Process Management
•A process is a program in execution. 
•A process needs certain resources, including CPU time, 
memory, files, and I/O devices, to accomplish its task.
The Following activities are, 
✦ Process creation and deletion.
✦ process suspension and resumption.
✦ Provision of mechanisms for:
–• process synchronization
–• process communication
6

ii. Main-Memory Management
•Memory is a large array of words or bytes, each with its 
own address. 
•It is a repository of quickly accessible data shared by the 
CPU and I/O devices.
•Main memory is a volatile storage device. 
•It loses its contents in the case of system failure.
•Following activities are, 
 Keep track of which parts of memory are currently 
being used and by whom.
 Decide which processes to load when memory space 
becomes available.
 Allocate and de-allocate memory space as needed.
7

iii. File Management
•A file is a collection of related information defined by its 
creator. Commonly, files represent programs and data.
Following activities are, 
✦ File creation and deletion.
✦ Directory creation and deletion.
✦Support of primitives for manipulating files and directories.
✦ Mapping files onto secondary storage.
✦ File backup on stable (non-volatile) storage media.
8

iv. I/O System Management
The I/O system consists of:
✦ A buffer-caching system
✦ A general device-driver interface
✦ Drivers for specific hardware devices
9

v. Secondary-Storage Management
•Main memory (primary storage) is volatile and too 
small to accommodate all data and programs 
permanently the computer system must provide 
secondary storage to back up main memory.
•the following activities are, 
✦ Free space management
✦ Storage allocation
✦ Disk scheduling
10

Types of Operating Systems
Operating systems can be classified into several types 
based on their functionality and usage.
•Batch, Time-Sharing, Distributed, 
•Network, 
•Real-time, Mobile, 
•Embedded operating systems.
Additionally, there are based on user count 
•single-user, 
•multi-user,
•processing capabilities (multiprogramming, 
multiprocessing, multitasking), and other factors.
11

1. Batch Operating System:
•Processes jobs in batches without direct user 
interaction.
•Suitable for tasks that can be completed offline, like 
payroll processing or generating reports.
2. Time-Sharing Operating System:
•Allows multiple users to access the system 
simultaneously, sharing resources like CPU time.
•Enables interactive computing, where users can run 
programs and receive feedback in real-time.
12

3. Distributed Operating System:
•Manages a collection of interconnected computers, 
presenting them as a single system.
•Facilitates resource sharing and communication 
between different machines.
4. Network Operating System:
•Runs on a server and manages network resources 
like files, printers, and users.
•Provides services for network communication, 
security, and administration.
13

5. Real-Time Operating System:
•Designed for systems where timing is critical, like 
industrial control or medical devices.
•Responds to events within a strict time constraint.
6. Mobile Operating System:
•Specifically designed for mobile devices like 
smartphones and tablets.
•Optimized for touch-based interfaces and mobile-
specific features.
14

7. Embedded Operating System:
•Used in specialized devices like appliances, vehicles, 
and industrial equipment.
•Typically lightweight and tailored to the specific 
hardware and functionality of the device.
15

Other important distinctions:
Single-user vs. Multi-user:
•Single-user OSes support only one user at a time, while 
multi-user OSes allow multiple users to access the 
system concurrently.
MultiProgramming vs. MultiTasking vs. 
MultiProcessing:
–Multiprogramming:Allows multiple programs to 
reside in memory and be executed concurrently, 
improving CPU utilization.
–Multitasking:Allows a single user to run multiple 
programs seemingly at the same time, giving the 
illusion of parallel execution.
–Multiprocessing:Uses multiple processors to execute 
tasks, significantly increasing processing power.
16

Generation of Operating System
•First Generation (1945-1955): 
    Vacuum Tubes and Plugboards
•Computers in this era were large and expensive, 
relying on vacuum tubes for computation.
•Operating systems were rudimentary or non-
existent, with manual loading of program code.
•Users interacted directly with the hardware using 
plugboards to input instructions.
17

2. Second Generation (1955-1965): Transistors and 
Batch Systems
•The invention of the transistor led to smaller, faster, 
and more reliable computers.
•Batch processing systems emerged, allowing users to 
submit jobs (programs and data) to be executed 
sequentially.
•Magnetic tapes were used for storage and 
input/output operations.
•Examples include early IBM systems and the 
introduction of OS/360.
18

3. Third Generation (1965-1980): Integrated Circuits 
and Multiprogramming
•The use of integrated circuits (ICs) resulted in further 
miniaturization and increased computing power.
•Multiprogramming allowed multiple programs to 
reside in memory and share the CPU, improving 
efficiency.
•Time-sharing systems allowed multiple users to 
interact with the computer simultaneously.
•Examples include the development of Unix and the 
rise of personal computers.
•Graphical User Interfaces (GUIs) began to emerge.
19

4. Fourth Generation (1980-Present): Personal 
Computers and Networking
•The invention of the microprocessor led to the 
development of personal computers.
•GUIs became more sophisticated, making computers 
more user-friendly.
•Networking technologies advanced, leading to the 
development of network operating systems.
•Examples: Windows, macOS, Linux, and Android.
•The focus shifted towards mobility and cloud 
computing.
20

OS Structure Methods
•OS Structure Methods is a design that enables user 
application programs (MSO, WB, MP, Comm.Player. Whatsapp, 
gmail etc..) to communicate with the hardware of the 
machine.
•Monolithic Structure
•Layered Approach Structure
•Modular Structure
•Micro-Kernel Structure
21

1. MONOLITHIC STRUCTURE
•The monolithic operating system controls all aspects of 
the operating system's operation, including file 
management, memory management, device 
management, and operational operations.
•The core part of OS is called the kernel (OS). 
•All other System components are provided with 
fundamental services by the kernel. 
•The OS and H/W use it as their main interface. 
22

23

•Monolithic kernel performs the role of a virtual 
computer. 
•Monolithic kernel is an old operating system that was 
used in banks to carry out simple tasks like batch 
processing and time-sharing, which allows numerous 
users at different terminals to access the Operating 
System.
24

2. LAYERED STRUCTURE
•The OS is separated into layers or levels in this kind of 
arrangement. 
•Layer 0 (the lowest layer) contains the hardware.
•layer 1 (the highest layer) contains the user interface 
(layer N). 
•These layers are organized view of hierarchically, with the 
top-level layers making use of the capabilities of the 
lower-level ones.
•The layered structures are hierarchical, debugging is 
simpler, so that, all lower-level layers are debugged 
before the upper layer is examined. 
25

LAYERED STRUCTURE
26

3. MICRO-KERNEL STRUCTURE
•Operating System using a micro-kernel framework 
that strips the kernel of any unnecessary parts. 
•Each Micro-Kernel is created separately and is kept 
apart from the others. 
•If one Micro-Kernel effect by malfunctions, the 
remaining operating system is unaffected and 
continues to function normally.
•As result, the system is now more trustworthy and 
secure. 
27

28

OS Services
There are five services,
i. 1. Program Execution
•The computer systems is to allow the user to execute 
programs.  
•So the operating system provides an environment 
where the user can conveniently run programs. 
•Running a program involves the allocating and de-
allocating memory, CPU scheduling in case of 
multiprocessing.
29

Cont...
2. I/O Operations
•Each program requires an input and produces 
output. 
•So, the operating systems are providing I/O makes it 
convenient for the users to run programs.
3. File System Manipulation
•The output of a program may need to be written into 
new files or input taken from some files. The 
operating system provides this service.
30

4. Communications
•The processes need to communicate with each other 
to exchange information during execution. 
•Communications may be between processes running 
on the same computer or running on the different 
computers. 
•Communications occur in two ways: 
–(i) shared memory
–(ii) message passing
31

Cont...
5. Error Detection
•An error is one part of the system may cause 
malfunctioning of the complete system. 
•To avoid such a situation operating system constantly 
monitors the system for detecting the errors. 
•There are three services provided by operating 
systems for ensuring the efficient operation of the 
system itself.
i. Resource allocation
ii. Accounting
iii. Protection
32

Cont...
i. Resource allocation
•When multiple users are logged on the system or 
multiple jobs are running at the same time, 
resources must be allocated to each of them. 
•Many different types of resources are managed by 
the operating system.
ii. Accounting
•The OS keep track of which users use how many and 
which kinds of computer resources.
•This record keeping may be used for accounting (so 
that users can be billed) or simply for accumulating 
usage statistics.
33

Cont...
iii. Protection
•When several disjointed processes execute 
concurrently, it should not be possible for one 
process to interfere with the others. 
•Protection involves ensuring that all access to system 
resources is controlled. 
•Security of the system from outsiders is also 
important. Such as username and password, to be 
allowed access to the resources.
34

1. System Call
❑ System calls provide an interface between the 
process and the operating system. 
❑System calls allow user-level processes to request 
some services from OS which process itself is not 
allowed to do. 
For example, I/O process involves a system call telling 
the OS to read or write particular area and this request 
is satisfied by the operating system.
35

Different types of system calls
1. Process control
• end, abort
• load, execute
• create process, terminate process
• get process attributes, set process attributes
• wait for time
• wait event, signal event
• allocate and free memory
36

2. File management
• create file, delete file
• open, close
• read, write, reposition
• get file attributes, set file attributes
3. Device management
• request device, release device
• read, write, reposition
• getdeviceattributes, set deviceattributes
• logically attach or detach devices
37

4. Information maintenance
• get time or date, set time or date
• get system data, set system data
• get process, file, or device attributes
• set process, file, or device attributes
5. Communications
• create, delete communication connection
• send, receive messages
• transfer status information
• attach or detach remote devices
38

2. System Programs
39
•System programs provide convenient environment 
for program development and execution.
•Its acting as a bridge between the UI and system 
call. 

Key Functions
1.File Management: A file is a collection of specific 
information stored in the memory of system. 
•Its defined as the process of manipulating files in the 
computer system.
•Its includes the process of creating, modifying and 
deleting files.
2.  Command Line Interface(CLI’s) :provide user facility to 
write commands directly to the system for performing 
any operation. 
•CLIs can perform many tasks like file manipulation, 
system configuration and etc.
40

Key Functions
3. Device Drivers: 
•it act as an intermediary between the OS and devices.
•Its provide facility to both OS and devices to understand 
each other’s language so that they can work together 
efficiently without interrupt.
4. Status Information: 
•Information like date, time amount of available memory, 
or disk space is asked by some users.
•All this information is formatted and displayed on output 
devices or printed. 
41

Key Functions
5. File Modification:
•This is used for modifying the content of files. 
•The files stored on disks or other storage devices, we use 
different types of editors. 
•For searching contents of files or perform 
transformations of files we use special commands.
6. Programming-Language support: 
•For common programming languages, we use Compilers, 
Assemblers, Debuggers, and interpreters which are 
already provided to users. 
42

Key Functions
7. Program Loading and Execution: 
•When the program is ready after Assembling and 
compilation, it must be loaded into memory for 
execution.
• A loader is part of an operating system that is 
responsible for loading programs and libraries. 
43

Key Functions
8. Communications: 
•Programs provides connections among processes, users, 
and computer systems.
•Users can send messages to another user on their screen.
•User can send e-mail, browsing on web pages, remote 
login, the transformation of files from one user to 
another.
44

3. System boot
45

3. System boot
46
•System boot isthe process of starting a computer or 
embedded device by loading the OS from disk into 
RAM.
•It's the sequence of events that occurs when you turn 
on a computer and it becomes ready for use.
•The boot process can be triggered by a power button 
press or a software command

Breakdown Of The Key Stages:
47
1.Power-on Self-Test (POST):
2.Bootloader Activation:
3.Operating System Loading:
4.System Initialization:
5.User Mode Initialization:
There are two main types of booting:
–Cold Booting:Starting the computer from a 
completely powered-off state.
–Warm Booting:Restarting the computer while it's 
already powered on.

Booting is crucial for:
48
•Transitioning from dormant to active state:Loading the 
OS allows the system to become usable.
•Applying software updates:The boot process allows the 
system to check for and install updates.
•Clearing memory:Some systems use booting to reset 
RAM.
•Recovery:Booting can help restore a system after a 
crash.
•Optimal resource allocation:Booting ensures hardware 
and software are properly initialized

4. Virtual Machines
49
•A virtual machine (VM) in an operating system provides 
an isolated environment that behaves like a separate 
computer.
•VM allowing multiple OS and applications to run 
concurrently on a single physical machine.
•This is achieved through a software layer called a 
hypervisor, which manages and allocates resources to 
each VM.

Two type of Virtual Machine,
50
1. System Virtual Machine:gives us complete system 
platform and gives the execution of the complete virtual 
OS.
2. Process Virtual Machine :While process virtual 
machines, unlike system virtual machine, does not provide 
us with the facility to install the virtual operating system 
completely.

Process Concept
1.  The Process: An OS executes a variety of programs 
that run as a process.
•Process – a program in execution; 
•process execution must progress in sequential fashion. 
•No parallel execution of instructions of a  single process
•Multiple parts
–The program code, also called text section
–Current activity including program counter, processor 
registers
–Stack containing temporary data
•Function parameters, return addresses, local variables

1. Process Concept(cont...)
–Data section containing global variables
–Heap containing memory dynamically allocated 
during run time.
•Program is passive entity stored on disk 
(executable file); process is active 
–Program becomes process when an executable file is 
loaded into memory
•Execution of program started via GUI mouse clicks 
or command line entry of its name, etc.
•One program can be several processes
–Consider multiple users executing the same program

Process in Memory

Memory Layout of a C Program

2. Process State

Process State
•As a process executes, it changes state
–New:  The process is being created
–Running:  Instructions are being executed
–Waiting:  The process is waiting for some event to 
occur
–Ready:  The process is waiting to be assigned to a 
processor
–Terminated:  The process has finished execution

3. Process Control Block (PCB)
•Process state – new, ready, running, 
waiting, etc.
•Program counter – location of 
instruction to next execute
•CPU registers – contents of all process-
centric registers
•CPU scheduling information- priorities, 
scheduling queue pointers
Information associated with each process(also called task 
control block)

Cont...
•Memory-management information – 
memory allocated to the process
•Accounting information – CPU used, 
clock time elapsed since start, time 
limits.
•I/O status information – I/O devices 
allocated to process, list of open files
Information associated with each process(also called task 
control block)

Cont ...
•PCB is data structure used to store all the information 
about a process. 
•When a process is created (initialized or installed), the 
OS creates a  corresponding process control block. 
•PCB includes CPU scheduling, I/O resource  
management, file management information etc. 
•By this technique, the hardware state can be restored 
so  that, the process can be scheduled to run again. 
•If that process gets suspended, the contents of the 
registers  are saved on a stack and the particular stack  
frame is stored in the PCB. 

Threads
•A thread is a single sequence stream within a 
process.
•Threads called lightweight processes. 
•Each thread belongs to exactly one process.
•OS supports multithreading, the process can consist 
of many threads.
•But threads can be effective only if the CPU is more 
than 1 otherwise two threads have to context 
switch for that single CPU.

Threads(cont...)
•A thread comprises of its 
own
–Thread ID –Unique ID for a 
thread in execution
–Program counter –Keeps track 
of instruction to execute
–System Register set –Active 
Variables of thread
–Stack –All execution history 
(Can be used for debugging)

Multithreading Models
•Threads may be provided either user level (user 
threads), or kernel level (kernel threads).
1. User threads
2. Kernel threads
•User Threads are supporting the kernel and manage 
without kernel support.
•Kernel Threads are support and manage directly by 
the operating system.

Multithreading Models(cont..)
•Three common ways of establishing such a 
relationship: 
1.one-to one model 
2.many-to-one model
3. many-to-many model.
1.one-to one model

1. One-to-One Model
•This model maps each user thread to a kernel 
thread. 
•It provides more concurrency than many-to-one 
model by allowing another thread to run when a 
thread makes a blocking system call. 
•It allows multiple threads to run in parallel on 
multiprocessors. 
•Drawback: kernel threads may burden the 
performance of a system. 

2. Many-to-One Model
•This model maps many user-level threads to one 
kernel thread. 
•Thread management is done by the thread library in 
user space, so it is efficient. 
•However, the entire process will block if a thread 
makes a blocking system call.

3. Many-to-Many Model
•The many-to-many model (many user-level threads to 
many kernel-level threads) avoids many of the 
limitations of the one-to-one model while extending 
multithreading capabilities even further.
•In this model, a user-level threads library provides 
sophisticated scheduling of user-level threads above 
kernel threads.

Scheduling Criteria
CPU utilization – 
•How efficiently the CPU is being used. 
•The goal is to keep the CPU as busy as possible.
Throughput –  
•Number of processes complete their execution per time 
unit.
•Higher throughput means better performance.
. 

Scheduling Criteria
Turnaround Time – 
•Total time taken from process submission to completion 
(waiting + execution + I/O time).
Waiting Time – 
•Time a process spends waiting in the ready queue. 
•Lower waiting time is desirable.
Response Time – 
•Amount of time it takes from when a request was 
submitted until the first response is produced. 

Scheduling Algorithms
–First- Come, First-Served (FCFS) Scheduling
–Shortest-Job-First (SJF) Scheduling
–Round Robin (RR)
–Priority Scheduling
–Multilevel Queue
–Multilevel Feedback Queue

Scheduling Algorithms (cont...)

2. Shortest-Job-First (SJF) Scheduling
•In SJF scheduling, the process with the 
lowest burst time, among the list of available 
processes in the ready queue, is going to be 
scheduled next.
•SJF is optimal – gives minimum average 
waiting time for a given set of processes
•Preemptive version called shortest-
remaining-time-first

Example of SJF

3. Round Robin (RR)
•Round Robin: where each process is cyclically 
assigned a fixed time slot. 
•Each process gets a small unit of CPU time (is called 
“time quantum q”).  
•Each process in the ready queue is assigned the CPU 
for that time quantum, if the execution of the 
process is completed then the process willendelse 
the process will go back to thewaiting tableand 
wait for its next turn to complete the execution.
•Its preemptive.

Example of RR with Time Quantum = 4

Time Quantum and Context Switch Time

4.Priority Scheduling
•A priority number (integer) is associated with each 
process
•The CPU is allocated to the process with the highest 
priority (smallest integer  highest priority)
–Preemptive
–Nonpreemptive
•SJF is priority scheduling where priority is the inverse of 
predicted next CPU burst time
•Problem  Starvation – low priority processes may 
never execute
•Solution  Aging – as time progresses increase the 
priority of the process

Example of Priority Scheduling

Priority Scheduling with Round-Robin
•Run the process with the highest priority. 
•Processes with the same priority run round-robin

5. Multilevel Queue
•The Ready Queue consists of multiple queues
•Multilevel queue scheduler defined by the following 
parameters:
–Number of queues
–Scheduling algorithms for each queue
–Method used to determine which queue a process 
will enter when that process needs service
–Scheduling among the queues

Multilevel Queue(cont...)
•Prioritization based upon process type

Multilevel Queue(cont...)
•With priority scheduling, have separate 
queues for each priority.
•Schedule the process in the highest-
priority queue!

6.Multilevel Feedback Queue

6.Multilevel Feedback Queue
Processes are assigned to different queues with 
varying priorities and scheduling algorithms.
Example:Multiple queues can be used, with the 
highest priority queue using FCFS and lower priority 
queues using Round Robin with different time slices.
–Processes can move between queues based on 
their behavior (e.g., CPU-bound or I/O-bound).
Key Feature:Highly flexible and adaptable to 
different workloads.

Process Synchronization
•Synchronization refers tothe coordination of 
multiple processes or threads to ensure they access 
shared resources in a controlled manner, preventing 
conflicts and maintaining data consistency.
•Synchronization mechanisms used to synchronize the 
processes, 
 When two or more process cooperates with each 
other, their order of execution must be preserved 
otherwise conflicts in their execution and 
inappropriate outputs can be produced.

Process Synchronization (cont...)
•A cooperative process can affect the execution of 
other process or can be affected by the execution of 
other process. 
•Such processes need to be synchronized so that their 
order of execution can be guaranteed.
•The procedure involved in preserving the appropriate 
order of execution of cooperative processes is known 
as Process Synchronization. 

Critical Section Problem
•Consider system of n processes {p
0 
, p
1 
, ... p
n-1
}
•Each process has critical section is code 
segment.
•Code  segment is shared variables..
•Eg: P1, p2, p3 all the process wants to use the 
common variable... is called as CSP.
–Process may be changing common variables, 
updating table, writing file, etc.
–When one process in critical section, no other may 
be in its critical section

Critical Section Problem
•Critical section problem is to design protocol 
to solve this..
•Each process must ask permission to enter 
critical section in entry section, may follow 
critical section with exit section, then 
remainder section.
•Entry Section: Controls the entry into CS and 
gets LOCK on required resources...
•Critical section: code segments
•Exit section: removes LOCK an resources and 
others know that its CS is over.

Critical Section
•General structure of process P
i 
(P1,P2, P3...)

Critical Section Problem(cont...)
1.Mutual Exclusion - If process P
i
 is executing in its 
critical section, then no other processes can be 
executing in their critical sections
2.Progress - If no process is executing in its critical 
section and wish to enter their critical section, then 
the selection of the process that will enter the critical 
section next cannot be postponed indefinitely.
solution to critical-section problem

Critical Section Problem(cont...)
3. Bounded Waiting - A bound must exist on the number 
of times that other processes are allowed to enter 
their critical sections after a process has made a 
request to enter its critical section and before that 
request is granted.
solution to critical-section problem

Issues of critical section
1.Deadlock:
•When two or more threads or processes wait for 
each other to release a critical section, It can result 
in a deadlock situation.
•Deadlocks can be difficult to detect and resolve, and 
its significant impact on a program’s performance 
and reliability.

Issues of critical section
2. Starvation:
•When a thread or process is repeatedly prevented 
from entering a critical section, So, thread or 
process is unable to progress. 
•This can happen if the critical section is held for an 
unusually long period of time, or if a high-priority 
thread or process is always given priority when 
entering the critical section.

Issues of critical section
3. Overhead:
•Overhead may reduce the program’s overall 
performance.
•When using critical sections, threads or processes 
must acquire and release locks or semaphores, 
which can take time and resources. 

Race Condition
Race Condition is a situation in which two or more 
processes are reading or writing shared variable 
(shared resource), and the result depends on the 
timing of how the processes are scheduled.
AIM: the results of a process should never be affected by 
other process “Winning a Race”..
–If both the processes can potentially write a 
variable at a given time, that situation is called 
deadlock.
–One process can potentially write a variable while 
another reads the variable.

Race Condition cont...

Race Condition cont...
Race Condition: unpredictable results, (prevent by 
locking method- synchronized method).
Race conditions in software: Air Traffic Signal, 
Railway Signaling, Multithreaded environment in 
JAVA etc..
Race Condition may occur in Critical Section..

Peterson’s Solution
•Peterson’s solution is software-based solution to 
the critical section problem..
•It provides a good algorithm to give solution for 
two process...
•Assume that ML instructions LOAD and STORE are 
atomic; that is cannot be interrupted..
•The two processes P0 and P1 share two variables:
–int turn;
–boolean flag[2];

Peterson’s Problem
 A software solution to Critical Section Problem
Process Pi, Pj
int turn;
boolean flag[2];
Structure of process P
i
do 
{
flag[i] =TRUE;
turn =j;
While(flag[j] && turn ==j);
          critical section
flag[i]= FALSE;
            Remainder section
}while (TRUE);

Peterson’s Solution
1. The variable turn indicates whose turn is to 
enter the critical section.  (if turn=I, then P
i
 
will enter in the critical section)
2. The flag array is used to indicate if ready to 
enter the critical section.
–Flag[i]= true implies that process Pi is ready!
–(The peterson’s algorithm is an humble algorithm, 
Pi will set turn=j and Pj will set turn=i)

Peterson’s Solution
•Peterson’s solution preserves all the three 
conditions:
•Mutual Exclusion: is assured only one process 
can access the critical section at any time.
•Progress: is also assured, as process outside 
the CS does not blocks others processes from 
entering the CS.
•Bounded Waiting: is preserved as every gets a 
fair chance.

Peterson’s Solution Revisited
•The effects of instruction reordering in Peterson’s 
Solution
•This allows both processes to be in their critical section 
at the same time!
•To ensure that Peterson’s solution will work correctly 
on modern computer architecture we must use 
Memory Barrier.

Semaphore
•Semaphore: A integer variable to synchronize the 
progress of interacting processes.
•Two standard operations, accessed via two atomic 
operations
1.wait() 
2. signal() 
–both operation maintain the busy waiting.
–Without waiting BUSY waiting.
•Entry to the critical section is controlled by wait 
operation.
•Exit from a critical region is taken care by Signal 
operation.

Wait and Signal Operations
1. wait () operation
•If argument S is zero, then no operation 
performed.
•If argument S is positive, then it decrements 
the value.  
Example: 
wait(S) 
{ 
 while (S <= 0)
 // busy wait 
 //ie) doesn’t operation;
 S--;  
}

2. The signal () operation is increments the value 
of its arguments S.
•If argument S is positive, then it decrements 
the value.  
signal() operation
signal(S) 
{ 
    S++;
}

Semaphore Usage 
Two synchronization tools
1. Counting semaphore – integer value can range over an 
unrestricted domain.
2. Binary semaphore – integer value can range only 
between 0 and 1
–Same as a mutex lock
–Can implement a counting semaphore S as a binary 
semaphore. 
–With semaphores we can solve various 
synchronization problems

Semaphore Implementation
•There are 2 type of variable 
–1. Wait
–2. Signal
•These two variable used to solve CSP’s.
•To acheive process Synchronization in multi processing 
environment...
•Doesn’t require BUSY WAITING.
•Must guarantee that NO two processes can execute  the 
wait() and signal() on the same semaphore at the same 
time.
•Thus, the implementation becomes the critical section 
problem where the wait and signal code are placed in the 
critical section.



Deadlock:
Deadlock may occur when two or more  
processes try to get the same multiple 
resources at a same time.

Starvation:
•Starvation is a problem of resource management, the 
process does not have resources because it is being 
used by other processes. 
•This problem occurs mainly in apriority-based 
scheduling algorithmin which the requests with high 
priority get processed first and the low priority 
processes get blocked for indefinite time.

Semaphore Implementation with no Busy waiting 
•With each semaphore there is an associated waiting 
queue
Each entry in a waiting queue has two data items:
– Value ( integer)
– Pointer to next record in the list
Two operations:
–block – place the process invoking the operation on 
the appropriate waiting queue
–wakeup – remove one of processes in the waiting 
queue and place it in the ready queue.

Semaphore Implementation with Busy waiting
                        

Implementation with no Busy waiting (Cont.)
wait(semaphore *S) { 
   S->value--; 
   if (S->value < 0) {
      add this process to S->list; 
      block(); 
   } 
}
signal(semaphore *S) { 
   S->value++; 
   if (S->value <= 0) {
      remove a process P from S->list; 
      wakeup(P); 
   } 
} 

Readers-Writers Problem
•A data set is shared among a number of concurrent 
processes.
–Readers : only read the data set; they do not perform 
any updates
–Writers   : can both read and write
•Problem – allow multiple readers to read at the same 
time.  
–Only one single writer can access the shared data 
at the same time.

Readers-Writers Problem (cont...)
•Shared Data
–Data set
–Semaphore mutex initialized to 1 (controls access to 
readcount)
–Semaphore wrt initialized to 1 (writer access)
–Integer readcount initialized to 0 (how many processes 
are reading object)

Readers-Writers Problem(cont..)
•The structure of a writer process 
              do {
                        wait (wrt) ;
                             //    writing is performed
                        signal (wrt) ;
             } while (TRUE);
       

Dining-Philosophers Problem
•Shared data 
–Bowl of rice (data set)
–Semaphore chopstick [5] initialized to 1

Dining-Philosophers Problem
• The K philosophers are seated around a circular table 
with one chopstick between each pair of philosophers. 
• There is one chopstick between each philosopher. 
• A philosopher may eat if he can pick up the two 
chopsticks adjacent to him.
• One chopstick may be picked up by any one of its 
adjacent followers but not both.

Dining-Philosophers Problem
•There are two states of the philosopher:THINKING 
and EATING. 
•Here two semaphores: Mutex and a semaphore array 
for the philosophers. 
•Mutex is used such that no two philosophers may 
access the pickup or put it down at the same time.
• Semaphore array is used to control the behavior of 
each philosopher. 
•But, semaphores can result in deadlock due to 
programming errors.

Dining-Philosophers Problem
Solution
•Mutual Exclusion Principle:No two Philosophers can have 
the two forks simultaneously.
•Free from Deadlock:Each philosopher can get the chance 
to eat in a certain finite time.
•Free from Starvation:When few Philosophers are waiting 
then one gets a chance to eat in a while.
•No strict Alternation
•Proper utilization of time

Deadlock
•Deadlockis a situation where a set of 
processes are blocked because each process is 
holding a resource and waiting for another 
resource acquired by some other process.
•A situation occurs in operating systems when 
there are two or more processes that hold 
some resources and wait for resources held by 
other(s).

Deadlock
For example,
•Process 1 is holding Resource 1 and waiting 
for resource 2 which is acquired by process 2, 
and process 2 is waiting for resource 1.

Deadlock Characterization
1. Necessary Condition
2. Resource -Allocation Graph

Deadlock Characterization
1. Necessary Condition
•Mutual exclusion:  only one thread at a time 
can use a resource( not sharable).
•Hold and wait:  a thread holding at least one 
resource is waiting to acquire additional 
resources held by other threads.
•No preemption:  a resource can be released 
only voluntarily by the thread holding it, after 
that thread has completed its task.
Deadlock can arise if four conditions hold simultaneously.

Deadlock Characterization
•Circular wait:
  
Deadlock can arise if four conditions hold simultaneously.

T

Deadlock can arise if four conditions hold simultaneously.

Graph with a Cycle But no Deadlock

Basic Facts
❑ If graph contains no cycles  no deadlock
❑ If graph contains a cycle 
–if only one instance per resource type,

possibility of deadlock

Methods for Handling Deadlocks
1.Ensure that the system will never enter a 
deadlock state:
–Deadlock prevention
–Deadlock avoidance
2.Allow the system to enter a deadlock 
state and then recover
3.Ignore the problem and pretend that 
deadlocks never occur in the system.

Deadlock Prevention
Deadlock prevention algorithms ensure that at 
least one of the necessary condition (Mutual 
Exclusion, Hold and wait, No preemption and 
circular wait) does not hold true.
Deadlock Prevention Deadlocks can be prevented 
by preventing at least one of the four required 
condition.

Deadlock Prevention
1.Mutual Exclusion: →  Sharable
     (more process trying to access single resource)
• Shared resources such as read only file do not 
lead to deadlocks.
•Unfortunately, some resources, such as printer and 
tap drives, require exclusive access by single 
process.

Deadlock Prevention (Cont.)
2. Hold and wait:
–Allocate all required resources to the process 
before the start of its execution this way Hold and 
Wait condition is eliminated.
Example: printer
–Allows process to request resources only when the 
process has None.
Disadvantages: 
–Low Resource Utilization
–Starvation

Deadlock Prevention (Cont.)
137
3. No Preemption: 
•if a process that is holding some resources 
requests another resource that cannot be 
immediately allocated to it, then all resources 
currently being held are Released.
•if a process requests resources, check if 
resource are available.
• if not available, allocated to some other 
process that is waiting (Forced).

Deadlock Prevention (Cont.)
138
4.Circular Wait:
➢ Assign numerical value to resources.
➢One way to avoid Circular Wait is to number 
all the resources, and to require that 
processes request resources only in strictly 
(increasing or decreasing) order.
Printer → 5
Disk → 7
Scanner → 11

Deadlock Avoidance
1.Safe State

Deadlock Avoidance
Deadlock avoidance in OS isa strategy to prevent 
deadlocks by carefully managing resource allocation, 
ensuring the system always remains in a safe state.
Basic Facts
– If a system is in safe state  no deadlocks.
– If a system is in unsafe state  possibility of   
     deadlock.
– Avoidance  ensure that a system will never 
    enter an unsafe state.

Safe, Unsafe, Deadlock State 

Key Concepts in Banker's Algorithm
•Safe State:There exists at least one sequence of 
processes such that each process can obtain the 
needed resources, complete its execution, release 
its resources, and allow other processes to 
eventually complete without entering a deadlock.
•Unsafe State:Even though the system can still 
allocate resources to some processes, there is no 
guarantee that all processes can finish without 
potentially causing a deadlock.

Avoidance Algorithms
•Single instance of a resource type
–Use a Resource-Allocation Graph
•Multiple instances of a resource type
– Use the Banker’s Algorithm

Resource-Allocation Graph

Unsafe State In Resource-Allocation Graph

3. Banker’s Algorithm
•Multiple instances of resources
•Each thread must a priori claim maximum use
•When a thread requests a resource, it may have 
to wait  
•When a thread gets all its resources it must 
return them in a finite amount of time

Data Structures for the Banker’s Algorithm 
•Available:  Vector of length m. If available [j] = k, 
there are k instances of resource type R
j  
available
•Max: n x m matrix.  If Max [i,j] = k, then process T
i
 
may request at most k instances of resource type 
R
j
•Allocation:  n x m matrix.  If Allocation[i,j] = k then 
T
i
 is currently allocated k instances of R
j
•Need:  n x m matrix. If Need[i,j] = k, then T
i
 may 
need k more instances of R
j 
to complete its task
Need [i,j] = Max[i,j] – Allocation [i,j]
